title: How Chalet Market Grew Organic Traffic 105% with Weekly Content
excerpt: How a Montana gourmet food retailer on Shopify grew organic traffic 105% in six months with weekly human-written content.
challenge:
Chalet Market is a family-owned business offering premium Montana meats, specialty foods, and gourmet items through their Shopify store.

Despite having exceptional products, their online traffic had stagnated. Their small team didn't have the time or resources to consistently create the content needed to improve search visibility and attract new customers.

Key challenges:
- Stagnant organic traffic
- Limited time for content creation
- Inconsistent publishing schedule
- Low search engine visibility
approach:
Starting in March 2024, our team built a customized content strategy informed by keyword research and audience insights. Real human writers produced weekly blog content tailored to Chalet Market's brand voice, Montana heritage, and gourmet product lines.

Five content types shipped on a weekly cadence:
- Product Spotlights: featured premium meats and specialty items with internal links and CTAs
- Brand Storytelling: connected Montana heritage and family values to product offerings
- Recipe Content: inspiring recipes using Chalet Market products
- Educational Articles: answered common customer questions about products and preparation
- Community Features: highlighted local Montana partnerships and suppliers

Every piece was optimized for search with strategic keyword placement, meta descriptions, internal linking, and structured formatting to maximize visibility.
results: Within six months, organic traffic to the Chalet Market Shopify store grew 105%. Holiday-level impressions arrived six months ahead of schedule. Customers spent more time browsing product pages, blog engagement quadrupled, and brand awareness deepened in their target market.
quote: Their content strategy has made a tangible difference in our online presence. We are seeing increased traffic to our website and Shopify store, and customers are engaging with our brand on a deeper level.
aeo_summary: Chalet Market is a family-owned Montana gourmet food retailer on Shopify. We built a weekly content engine of blog posts, product spotlights, brand storytelling, and recipe content, with on-page SEO across every piece. Organic traffic grew 105% in six months, blog engagement quadrupled, and holiday-level impressions arrived a half-year early.
seo_title: Chalet Market Case Study Organic Traffic Up 105 Percent in Six Months | Content Cucumber
seo_description: How Chalet Market, a Montana gourmet food retailer on Shopify, grew organic traffic 105% in six months with weekly human-written content from Content Cucumber.

title: BigCommerce's VIP Supply Co. Migration Story
excerpt: Case study we wrote for BigCommerce. Covers VIP Supply Co.'s 4-month migration from Shopify to BigCommerce B2B Edition, serving 1,800+ restaurants with 1,100+ SKUs.
content:
Read the published version on BigCommerce: bigcommerce.com/case-study/vip-supply-co/

Content Cucumber wrote this customer case study for BigCommerce's prospect-facing content library. We interviewed VIP Supply Co. leadership and Access Solutions (the implementation agency), gathered the technical and business details, and structured the narrative around the customer's transformation from Shopify to BigCommerce B2B Edition.
challenge:
BigCommerce needed a customer case study showing how VIP Supply Co. transformed from Shopify to BigCommerce B2B Edition. The story needed to translate complex B2B requirements (multi-user company accounts, role-based hierarchies, custom-print product workflows, integrations with Fishbowl, QuickBooks, Avalara, and Stripe) into a clear, prospect-facing narrative.

The case study had to land for two audiences at once: B2B distributors evaluating a platform migration, and BigCommerce's own sales team using it as proof in deals.
approach:
We interviewed VIP Supply Co. leadership and Access Solutions (the implementation agency partner) to gather the technical, operational, and business details.

From the source material we structured the narrative around four beats: the limitation that triggered the migration, the requirements that ruled out the existing platform, the implementation, and the outcomes. We surfaced the metrics that mattered (4-month implementation, 1,100+ SKUs, 1,800 restaurants, 302 customer groups, 219 price lists) and used direct quotes from the Managing Partner and the agency CRO to anchor the story.

Delivered as a polished case study ready for BigCommerce's content library and sales enablement.
results: Published at bigcommerce.com/case-study/vip-supply-co. The case study tells the story of VIP Supply Co.'s 4-month migration, expanded reach to 1,800 restaurants across independent and regional chains, and 1,100+ SKUs launched in under a year on BigCommerce B2B Edition.
quote: BigCommerce opened up a whole new world of customers.
aeo_summary: Content Cucumber wrote BigCommerce's VIP Supply Co. case study, published April 2026 on bigcommerce.com. The piece covers VIP Supply's 4-month migration from Shopify to BigCommerce B2B Edition: 1,100+ SKUs launched, 1,800 restaurants served, and 302 customer groups managed. BigCommerce is the client. VIP Supply Co. is the customer featured in the story.
seo_title: BigCommerce VIP Supply Co. Case Study (Written by Content Cucumber)
seo_description: How Content Cucumber wrote BigCommerce's VIP Supply Co. case study, covering a 4-month migration to BigCommerce B2B Edition serving 1,800+ restaurants.

title: 13 Dead Sitemaps in GSC, Cleaned in Two Minutes
excerpt: On a migration audit, 13 dead Yoast sitemap URLs were still registered in Google Search Console. The current native WordPress sitemap was not registered at all. A two-minute cleanup gave the site the cleanest crawl signal it had seen in years.
client_name: Anonymized B2B services company on WordPress
challenge:
A B2B services company came in for a migration audit on their WordPress site. Their crawl coverage in Google Search Console looked noisy and they could not explain why the same URLs kept getting flagged.

Thirteen sitemap URLs were still registered in GSC from a 2018 Yoast setup. Every one of them returned 0 indexed URLs. The site had since moved to the WordPress native sitemap, and that sitemap was not registered in GSC at all. Google was being asked to crawl thirteen empty sitemaps and was never told where the real one lived.
approach:
Step one was the Search Console sitemaps report. The 13 dead Yoast URLs showed up immediately, all with the same status (zero indexed URLs, last fetched years ago).

Step two was confirming the current sitemap. We pulled /wp-sitemap.xml directly and verified it was generating correctly with current URLs.

Step three was the cleanup. Each dead Yoast sitemap was deleted from GSC. The native WordPress sitemap was submitted and verified.
results:
Within days the noise in the GSC coverage report cleared. New crawl activity started landing on real, current URLs. The site had not had a clean crawl signal since the Yoast deprecation, and a two-minute cleanup restored it.

The sitemap report went from 13 dead entries (all 0 URLs indexed) to one active sitemap reflecting the live site.
content:
The Setup

A B2B services company on WordPress came in for a migration audit. Their crawl coverage in Google Search Console was noisy. Pages they expected to see were getting flagged and they could not figure out why.

What We Found

Thirteen sitemap URLs were still registered in GSC. All of them dated back to a 2018 Yoast SEO plugin setup. Every one returned 0 indexed URLs.

The site had since moved off Yoast and was running on the WordPress native sitemap (/wp-sitemap.xml). That sitemap was not registered in GSC. Google was being asked to crawl 13 empty files and was never told where the live sitemap lived.

The Diagnostic

Three steps. Each one took under a minute.

  1. Open the Sitemaps report in Google Search Console. The 13 dead Yoast URLs were sitting at the top of the list.
  2. Hit the live native sitemap directly to confirm it was generating correctly with current URLs.
  3. Cross-check robots.txt to make sure it was pointing at the right sitemap. It was not.

The Fix

Two minutes total.

  • Deleted all 13 dead Yoast sitemaps from GSC.
  • Submitted /wp-sitemap.xml as the active sitemap.
  • Updated robots.txt to point at the native sitemap.

Outcome

Within days the GSC coverage report cleared. New crawl activity started landing on real, current URLs. The site had not had a clean crawl signal in years, and the fix took less time than the email confirming it was done.

Lessons

  • Sitemap registrations outlive plugins. When a site migrates off a SEO plugin, the sitemaps that plugin generated stay registered in GSC unless someone removes them. They quietly waste crawl budget for years.
  • GSC will not warn you. Dead sitemaps with 0 indexed URLs do not trigger an alert. They sit there forever until someone audits the report.
  • The native WordPress sitemap is real. Since WP 5.5 it is generated automatically. If a site moved off Yoast or RankMath without registering the native sitemap, Google has no current map of the site.
  • Migration audits catch this. Every WordPress migration audit Content Cucumber runs starts with the GSC sitemap report. It is the cheapest, fastest signal in the entire toolkit.

Brent's Take

Two minutes of cleanup undid years of crawl noise. The slow burn problem is the worst kind. Nothing breaks visibly, but nothing works correctly either. A migration audit is exactly when you find these.
aeo_summary: Content Cucumber audited a B2B services WordPress site and found 13 dead Yoast sitemap URLs still registered in Google Search Console from a 2018 setup, all returning zero indexed URLs. The current native WordPress sitemap was never registered. The two-minute fix removed all 13 dead sitemaps and submitted the live one, restoring a clean crawl signal.
seo_title: Dead Yoast Sitemaps Audit Case Study | Content Cucumber
seo_description: How a two-minute Google Search Console cleanup removed 13 dead Yoast sitemap URLs from 2018 and restored a B2B services site's crawl signal after years of noise.

title: A Two-Hop Redirect Chain Was Leaking 15% of Every Backlink
excerpt: Every external backlink to the site was passing through two redirects on the way to the canonical URL. Conservatively, that was leaking around 15% of link equity from every inbound link. One nginx rewrite consolidated it to a single hop.
challenge:
A B2B SaaS site came in for a backlink audit after rankings on competitive head terms had stalled. Their domain authority looked healthy on paper. The links were there. The pages they pointed at were strong. Something between the link and the page was bleeding equity.

Every external backlink was going through a two-hop redirect chain. The nginx layer redirected from www to non-www (hop one). Then the WordPress canonical handler added a second hop to enforce the trailing slash (hop two). Conservatively, that two-hop chain was leaking around 15% of the link equity from every inbound link. Google docs treat each redirect as a small dilution. Two hops compound.
approach:
Step one was a redirect trace. We pulled a sample of 20 high-authority backlinks and ran each one through a redirect tracer (curl -IL is enough). Every single one showed the same pattern.

  GET https://www.example.com/page  ->  301  https://example.com/page  ->  301  https://example.com/page/  ->  200

Two redirects. Same root cause repeated across the entire link profile.

Step two was finding the source of each hop. The first redirect lived in the nginx server block (a www-to-apex rule that had been in place since launch). The second lived inside WordPress, where the canonical handler enforces trailing slashes on permalinks.

Step three was consolidating both decisions into the nginx layer. One rewrite rule went from www-without-slash directly to apex-with-slash, in a single 301.
results:
Every external backlink now resolves in one hop instead of two. The conservative estimate of recovered link equity is around 15% per inbound link. On a site with hundreds of referring domains, that is a meaningful share of the link profile no longer being thrown away.

The fix took one nginx config change and a reload. No content moved, no URLs changed for users.
content:
The Setup

A B2B SaaS company came in for a backlink audit. Their rankings on competitive head terms had stalled. Domain authority looked healthy on paper. The links were there. The pages those links pointed at were strong. Something between the inbound link and the live page was leaking value.

What We Found

Every external backlink to the site was going through a two-hop redirect chain.

GET https://www.example.com/page
  -> 301 https://example.com/page
  -> 301 https://example.com/page/
  -> 200 OK

Two hops, same root cause repeated across hundreds of referring domains. Google's documentation treats each redirect as a small equity loss. Two redirects compound. Conservatively, that was around 15% of link equity bleeding off every single inbound link.

The Diagnostic

Three steps.

  1. Redirect trace on a sample of high-authority backlinks. A simple curl -IL against twenty referring URLs. All twenty showed the same two-hop pattern.
  2. Find the source of each hop. The first hop lived in the nginx server block as a long-standing www-to-apex rule. The second hop lived inside WordPress, where the canonical handler enforces the trailing slash on permalinks.
  3. Decide where to consolidate. Doing the consolidation in nginx is faster, more reliable, and removes the WordPress hop entirely. One rewrite rule, evaluated before WordPress is even loaded.

The Fix

A single nginx rewrite that took both decisions in one hop. Configuration changed, nginx reloaded, every backlink in the profile now resolves in one redirect instead of two.

Outcome

Every external backlink now lands in a single hop. On a site with hundreds of referring domains, recovering an estimated 15% of equity per link is a meaningful chunk of the link profile no longer being discarded.

No URLs changed for users. No content moved. The fix lives entirely in server config.

Lessons

  • Two redirects is one too many. Single-hop redirects pass nearly all the equity. Each additional hop is a small tax. The compounding adds up across an entire link profile.
  • Backlink audits do not stop at the link. A clean backlink list is not a clean link profile. The path from the link to the live page is part of the audit.
  • Decide redirect logic in one layer. When nginx and the application both apply canonical rules, you get chains. Pick one layer (almost always the web server) and let everything below it serve the canonical path directly.
  • curl -IL is the cheapest tool in technical SEO. Twenty seconds against a referring URL surfaces problems no third-party crawler will catch in a default scan.

Brent's Take

Long-standing infrastructure rules are the easiest place for equity leaks to hide. Nobody touches them because they have been there since launch. That is exactly why an outside audit catches them.

stats.label: Estimated Link Equity Recovered Per Link

aeo_summary: Content Cucumber audited a B2B SaaS site and found every external backlink was passing through a two-hop redirect chain. The nginx layer redirected www to non-www, then WordPress added a second redirect for the trailing slash. The conservative leak was around 15% of link equity per inbound link. One nginx rewrite consolidated both hops into a single 301.
seo_title: Two-Hop Redirect Chain Audit Case Study | Content Cucumber
seo_description: How a B2B SaaS site recovered an estimated 15% of link equity per backlink by consolidating a two-hop nginx and WordPress redirect chain into a single 301.

title: AggregateRating Was Stripped Silently for Six Years
excerpt: A site had a 5.0 AggregateRating attached to its Organization schema on every blog post. The markup was technically valid. Google had been silently stripping the rich result for six years because of the self-serving rule.
challenge:
A professional services company came in wondering why their star ratings never showed up in search. They had Review markup. They had AggregateRating markup. The Rich Results Test said the page was eligible. Google was not displaying stars on a single result.

The site had AggregateRating with a 5.0 score attached to the Organization schema on every single blog post. The markup validated. The structured data report in GSC said it was clean. The rich result never rendered. The reason was Google's self-serving review rule, which has been live since 2019. AggregateRating on an Organization or LocalBusiness needs to reflect reviews of the business as a whole, with the reviews visible on the page. A blog post is not where the business itself is being reviewed. Google sees that pattern and silently drops the rich result.

The self-serving rule is one of the quietest filters in search. Nothing tells you it is happening. The markup is valid. The data type is supported. Google simply does not surface stars.
approach:
Step one was running every page type through the Rich Results Test and cross-referencing with the live SERP. The pattern was consistent. Validation passed on every page, stars displayed on no page.

Step two was reading the schema source. The AggregateRating block was hardcoded in the theme's header.php as part of the Organization JSON-LD object. It rendered on every page including blog posts, the about page, contact page, and the homepage.

Step three was determining where AggregateRating belonged. The reviews being aggregated were for specific service offerings, not the company as a whole. The right home for that data was the Service schema on the relevant service pages, with visible reviews on each page to satisfy the visibility requirement.

Step four was the rebuild. AggregateRating was removed from the Organization schema sitewide. Per-service AggregateRating blocks were added to each service page, each tied to the reviews displayed on that page.
results:
Within Google's normal indexing cycle, star ratings began appearing on the service pages where they belonged. The rich result that had been silently suppressed for six years was finally rendering, on the pages where it was relevant.

The blog posts no longer carried inflationary 5.0 ratings they had no claim to. The site picked up rich-result eligibility on the pages that drive revenue (services), instead of on pages that were never going to convert anyway (blog).
content:
The Setup

A professional services company came in asking why their star ratings never showed up in search. They had Review markup. They had AggregateRating markup. The Rich Results Test confirmed the page was eligible. Google never displayed stars on a single result.

What We Found

AggregateRating with a 5.0 score was attached to the Organization schema on every blog post. The markup was technically valid. The structured data report in Google Search Console said it was clean. The rich result was being stripped silently.

The reason is Google's self-serving review rule, in place since 2019. AggregateRating on an Organization or LocalBusiness needs to reflect reviews of the business as a whole, and the reviews being aggregated need to be visible on the page. A blog post is not where the business is being reviewed. Google sees that pattern and silently drops the rich result.

This rule is one of the quietest filters in search. Nothing tells you it is happening. The markup validates. The data type is supported. Google simply does not surface stars.

The Diagnostic

Four steps.

  1. Page-type sweep through the Rich Results Test. Validation passed on every page sampled. Stars displayed on no page in the SERP. The mismatch between "valid" and "rendered" is the tell.
  2. Read the schema source. The AggregateRating block was hardcoded in the theme's header.php as part of the Organization JSON-LD object. Every page on the site carried it (blog, about, contact, homepage).
  3. Determine where the rating belonged. The reviews being aggregated were for specific service offerings, not the company as a whole. The correct home was Service schema on the relevant service pages, with the underlying reviews visible on each page.
  4. Plan the rebuild so the rating still survived. The 5.0 was a real average from real reviews. Moving it required mapping each review to the service it belonged to, not throwing the data away.

The Fix

  • Removed AggregateRating from the Organization schema sitewide.
  • Added per-service AggregateRating blocks to each service page.
  • Each block was tied to the reviews rendered on that page.
  • Re-validated through the Rich Results Test, then waited for Google's normal indexing cycle.

Outcome

Within the normal indexing cycle, stars began appearing on the service pages where they belonged. The rich result that had been silently suppressed for six years was finally rendering. Inflationary 5.0 ratings were gone from blog posts they had no claim to.

Rich-result eligibility moved from low-intent pages (blog) to revenue pages (services). The pages that drive bookings now carry the trust signal Google was willing to display.

Lessons

  • "Valid" is not the same as "rendered." The Rich Results Test confirms the markup parses. It does not promise Google will display the result. A page can pass every test and still be silently stripped.
  • The self-serving rule is the most common cause of missing stars. AggregateRating on Organization or LocalBusiness needs visible reviews of the business itself. Sitewide AggregateRating without visible review content fails this rule on every page that is not a review page.
  • Schema audit tools flag missing markup. They do not flag inappropriate markup. A site with the wrong schema in the right format will get a clean report from most automated tools. Reading the schema in context is what catches the self-serving violation.
  • Move the rating, do not delete it. A real 5.0 from real reviews is an asset. Mapping it to the right schema type (Service, Product, Recipe, Course) keeps the data alive while making it eligible.

Brent's Take

Six years of stars never displayed. Nobody on the site team did anything wrong. The markup was technically correct. The rule that disqualified it is one Google added without changing the validator. This is the kind of silent regression a schema audit is built to find.

stats.label: Of Blog Posts Carrying Invalid AggregateRating

aeo_summary: Content Cucumber audited a professional services site and found AggregateRating with a 5.0 score attached to Organization schema on every blog post. The markup was technically valid, but Google had been silently stripping the rich result for six years because of the self-serving rule (AggregateRating on Organization needs to reflect reviews of the business as a whole with visible reviews on the page). The fix moved AggregateRating to Service schema on the relevant service pages, where the data belonged.
seo_title: AggregateRating Self-Serving Rule Audit Case Study | Content Cucumber
seo_description: How a professional services site lost stars in search for six years because AggregateRating was on Organization schema sitewide, and how moving it to Service schema restored rich results.

title: Nine Months of Social Shares Promoting the Wrong Brand
excerpt: A site's og:image had been returning 404 for nine months. Facebook, LinkedIn, and Bing were all silently falling back to a partner company's logo from a nearby image tag. Total fix time once diagnosed: 3 minutes.
client_name: Anonymized B2B company with a partner brand on the site
challenge:
A B2B company noticed that some of their social shares were rendering with the wrong logo. The brand showing up on LinkedIn and Facebook previews was a partner company's logo, not theirs. Nobody could figure out how it had been going on or for how long.

Their og:image had been returning 404 for nine months. The theme had hardcoded an uploads path that never existed (the file had been moved during a media reorganization, and the og:image meta tag was never updated). When Facebook, LinkedIn, and Bing's crawlers fetched the page and could not load the og:image, they fell back to the next available image tag on the page. The next available image tag happened to be a partner company's logo in the footer. For nine months, every share of the site on every social network had been displaying that partner's logo as the brand.

Nobody had caught it because the platforms do not warn you. A 404 og:image silently degrades to whatever image the platform can find. The site looked fine to humans visiting it. Only people who shared a link saw the problem, and most of them assumed it was the platform's fault.
approach:
Step one was running the live site through three social share previewers (Facebook Sharing Debugger, LinkedIn Post Inspector, X Card Validator). All three returned the same fallback image. None of them flagged the og:image as broken. They just showed the next-best image they could find.

Step two was inspecting the page source. The og:image meta tag pointed at a hardcoded path: /wp-content/uploads/2024/social-default.jpg. A direct request to that URL returned 404.

Step three was tracing why. The site had been through a media library reorganization. The image was moved into a different folder structure. The og:image path in the theme was never updated. Nine months passed.

Step four was finding the right image and updating the path. Three minutes from diagnosis to fix. Push, scrape, verify.
results:
All three previewers immediately picked up the corrected og:image after a manual rescrape. Every share thereafter rendered with the correct brand. Facebook's cached fallback was forced to refresh through the Sharing Debugger. LinkedIn's cache cleared on its own within hours.

Nine months of social shares promoting the wrong brand stopped within an hour of diagnosis.
content:
The Setup

A B2B company noticed something off about their social shares. The brand rendering on LinkedIn and Facebook previews was not theirs. It was a partner company's logo. They asked us to figure out what was happening and how long it had been going on.

What We Found

The og:image had been returning 404 for nine months.

The theme had a hardcoded uploads path that no longer existed. The file had been moved during a media library reorganization. The og:image meta tag was never updated to match. When Facebook, LinkedIn, and Bing crawled the page and could not load the og:image, they fell back to the next available image tag. That next image was a partner company's logo in the footer. Every share for nine months rendered with that partner's logo as the brand.

Nobody caught it because the platforms do not warn you. A broken og:image silently degrades to whatever the platform can scrape. The site looked correct to anyone visiting it. Only people who shared a link saw the wrong logo, and most assumed the platform was at fault.

The Diagnostic

Four steps. The whole audit took fifteen minutes.

  1. Run the live site through three previewers. Facebook Sharing Debugger, LinkedIn Post Inspector, X Card Validator. All three returned the same fallback image. None of them flagged the og:image as broken. They simply rendered the best image they could find.
  2. Inspect the page source. The og:image meta tag pointed at /wp-content/uploads/2024/social-default.jpg. A direct request to that URL returned 404.
  3. Trace the cause. A media library reorganization had moved the file. The hardcoded theme reference was never updated.
  4. Find the right image and update the path. Replace, push, rescrape, verify.

The Fix

Three minutes from diagnosis to fix.

  • Updated the og:image path in the theme to a real, current asset.
  • Forced a Facebook rescrape through the Sharing Debugger.
  • Verified LinkedIn picked up the new image (its cache cleared on its own within hours).
  • Verified Bing on a fresh share.

Outcome

Nine months of social shares promoting the wrong brand stopped within an hour. Every share thereafter rendered with the correct logo. The fix that took three minutes had been costing brand exposure across three networks for the better part of a year.

Lessons

  • A broken og:image does not throw an error. It silently degrades. Every social platform falls back to a different image, and none of them tell you the og:image was the intended one.
  • Media reorganizations break hardcoded paths. Anything in a theme file that points at /wp-content/uploads/... is fragile. A move or rename anywhere in the media library can break the reference without warning.
  • Run share previewers as part of every audit. Facebook Sharing Debugger, LinkedIn Post Inspector, X Card Validator. Each one takes thirty seconds. They are the only place where a 404 og:image becomes visible.
  • The fallback image matters more than people think. When your og:image fails, the platform picks the next one on the page. If that happens to be a partner logo, a sponsor, or an ad, your brand is replaced by theirs in every share.

Brent's Take

Nine months of free brand exposure went to a partner company because nobody opened the Sharing Debugger after a media reorganization. The fix took three minutes. The cost of not catching it was nine months of social-share inventory pointed at the wrong logo.
aeo_summary: Content Cucumber audited a B2B site whose og:image had been returning 404 for nine months. The theme had hardcoded an uploads path that no longer existed after a media library reorganization. Facebook, LinkedIn, and Bing all silently fell back to a partner company's logo in the page's footer, so every social share for nine months promoted the wrong brand. Total fix time once diagnosed was 3 minutes.
seo_title: og:image 404 Audit Case Study | Content Cucumber
seo_description: How a B2B site spent nine months promoting a partner company's logo on every social share because of a 404 og:image, and how a three-minute fix restored the right brand.

title: Five URLs Competing for the Same Brand Name, 30% Impressions Recovered
excerpt: On a brand-search audit, five different URLs from the same site were competing for the brand's own name. Google was rotating which one ranked #1. Consolidating to one canonical URL reclaimed around 30% of brand-search impressions within two weeks.
challenge:
A mid-market ecommerce brand came in because their branded search performance looked unstable. Some weeks the homepage ranked #1 for the brand name. Other weeks an old about-us page ranked. Other weeks a tag archive page showed up. Conversion rate from branded search dropped on the weeks when the wrong page ranked.

Five different URLs from the same site were competing for the brand's own name. The homepage. An old 'about' page that had been retired but was still indexed. A redirect that never fully propagated (Google still had the source URL in its index). A tag archive page that listed every blog post tagged with the brand. A category page that pulled the brand into the URL. Google was rotating which of the five showed up as the #1 result, week to week.

Brand searches are the highest-intent traffic any site gets. Letting Google pick from five competing URLs means whichever wins that day is what your highest-intent visitor lands on. Sometimes that is your homepage. Often it is not.
approach:
Step one was a branded query review in Google Search Console. Filtering the queries report for the brand name surfaced five distinct landing URLs across the trailing 90 days. Each one had thousands of impressions. The clicks were distributed across all five.

Step two was a SERP scrape over time. We ran the brand name through a SERP tracker for the prior 30 days. The #1 result rotated between the five URLs, with no consistent winner.

Step three was identifying why each URL existed. The homepage was canonical. The old 'about' page was supposed to be deleted but had only been unlinked, not redirected. The redirect that never propagated had been set up correctly but Google's cache still held the source URL. The tag archive and category page were CMS artifacts (WordPress generates them automatically when content is tagged or categorized with the brand name).

Step four was the consolidation plan. Redirect the old 'about' page to the homepage. Force a recrawl of the never-propagated redirect through GSC URL Inspection. Either deindex the tag archive and category page (preferred) or canonicalize them to the homepage. Ensure internal links pointed only at the homepage for the brand term.
results:
Within two weeks, brand-search impressions to the canonical homepage recovered around 30%. Google stopped rotating the #1 result and consistently surfaced the homepage. Branded conversion rate stabilized.

The tag archive and category page were deindexed via robots meta. The old 'about' page redirected. Google's cache caught up on the never-propagated redirect after a forced recrawl. The five-way competition collapsed into one canonical destination.
content:
The Setup

A mid-market ecommerce brand came in because their branded search performance looked unstable. Some weeks the homepage ranked #1 for their brand name. Other weeks an old about page ranked. Other weeks a tag archive showed up. Conversion rate dropped on the weeks when the wrong page ranked.

Branded search is the highest-intent traffic a site gets. Whichever URL wins on a given day is what the highest-intent visitor lands on. Sometimes that was the homepage. Often it was not.

What We Found

Five different URLs from the same site were competing for the brand's own name.

  1. Homepage. The intended canonical destination.
  2. An old "about" page. Retired internally but still indexed because nobody redirected it.
  3. A redirect that never fully propagated. Set up correctly, but Google's cache still held the source URL.
  4. A tag archive. WordPress had auto-generated it from a tag using the brand name.
  5. A category page. Same root cause as the tag archive, different CMS taxonomy.

Google was rotating which of the five showed up as #1. The rotation wasted brand-search impressions on URLs that did not convert.

The Diagnostic

Four steps.

  1. GSC branded query review. Filtering the queries report for the brand name across 90 days surfaced five distinct landing URLs. Each one had thousands of impressions. Clicks were distributed across all five.
  2. SERP rotation tracking. A 30-day SERP scrape showed the #1 result rotating between the five URLs with no consistent winner.
  3. Source attribution per URL. Why does each URL exist? Which is canonical, which is an artifact, which is a leftover?
  4. Consolidation plan. A redirect map, a deindex list, and an internal-link cleanup pass.

The Fix

  • Redirected the old "about" page to the homepage with a 301.
  • Forced a recrawl of the never-propagated redirect through GSC URL Inspection. Google's cache caught up within a week.
  • Deindexed the tag archive and the category page via robots meta. The brand name no longer needed its own auto-generated taxonomy pages.
  • Audited internal links and pointed every brand-term anchor at the homepage. No more linking from blog posts to the tag archive.

Outcome

Within two weeks, brand-search impressions to the canonical homepage recovered around 30%. Google stopped rotating the #1 result and consistently surfaced the homepage. Branded conversion rate stabilized.

The five-way competition collapsed into one canonical destination. The wasted impressions on tag archives and orphan pages came back to where they could convert.

Lessons

  • Brand-search cannibalization is invisible until you look at it directly. Aggregate metrics for the brand name look fine. The problem only shows up when you attribute clicks per landing URL.
  • WordPress generates competing URLs automatically. Tag archives and category pages for brand-name taxonomies will rank if the site has enough authority. Either deindex them or do not tag with the brand name.
  • Unlinking is not removing. An old page that no longer has internal links is still indexed. Redirect it or deindex it. Otherwise Google can keep ranking it for years.
  • Redirects can fail silently in Google's cache. A 301 set up server-side does not always propagate immediately. URL Inspection plus a recrawl request is the fastest way to force the cache update.
  • Audit the internal-link graph. External backlinks are visible. Internal links are how a CMS quietly votes for the wrong URL. Cleaning up internal links to point at the canonical destination is half the fix.

Brent's Take

The fix list looks technical, but the real lesson is simpler. The site was casting five votes for itself on every brand search and letting Google pick the winner. A canonical URL is one vote, repeated everywhere. That is what stable brand-search performance looks like.
aeo_summary: Content Cucumber audited a mid-market ecommerce brand's branded search performance and found five different URLs from the same site competing for the brand's own name (homepage, an old about page, a redirect that never propagated, a tag archive, and a category page). Google was rotating the #1 result week to week. Consolidating to a single canonical URL through redirects, deindexing, and internal-link cleanup reclaimed around 30% of brand-search impressions within two weeks.
seo_title: Brand-Search Cannibalization Audit Case Study | Content Cucumber
seo_description: How a mid-market ecommerce brand reclaimed around 30% of brand-search impressions in two weeks by consolidating five competing URLs into one canonical homepage.
